danger_signals.visual_form: Increasing density of hostile characters on screen; varied enemy types advancing toward player
danger_signals.meaning: Active combat zone; escalating threat level as waves intensify

danger_signals.visual_form: Rundown streets, boarded buildings, damaged storefronts in Wood Oak City areas
danger_signals.meaning: The Syndicate has retaken control; lawlessness and criminal dominance in city zones

danger_signals.visual_form: Visually distinct, larger character models; unique animation sets; arena transitions
danger_signals.meaning: Peak danger encounter; story-critical combat scenario

danger_signals.visual_form: Adam Hunter shown chained to wall in photograph attached to door; island fortress setting
danger_signals.meaning: Hostage situation; personal stakes and moral urgency for protagonists

safety_signals.visual_form: On-ground collectibles (health restores, temporary power-ups, melee weapons); visual glow or distinct sprite appearance
safety_signals.meaning: Resource availability; player agency in combat preparation

safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple playable characters on screen simultaneously; two-player co-op mode enabled
safety_signals.meaning: Mutual support and reduced isolation; narrative emphasis on teamwork against common threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Level completion, transitions to new environments, visual stage clear indicators
safety_signals.meaning: Forward momentum; successful resistance against Syndicate presence

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Diverse enemy types reflecting Syndicate foot soldiers (thugs, brawlers, specialized combatants); varied character designs indicating organizational hierarchy
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Rundown urban zones; boarded buildings; Wood Oak City streets under Syndicate control; Mr. X's desolate island fortress hideout

faction_indicators.faction: Player characters / Resistance (Axel Stone, Blaze Fielding, Max Thunder, Skate Hunter)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Four distinct character models with unique animations, move sets, and visual silhouettes (Max as wrestler, Skate with rollerblades, Axel and Blaze with martial training backgrounds)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Starting point: nightspot/safe zone in Wood Oak City; journey from city streets to island; helicopter departure (rescue victory point)

faction_indicators.faction: Police force / Civic authority (Adam Hunter's affiliation)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Adam's house (civilian/family space); Small house setting contrasts with Syndicate-dominated streets; represents legitimate authority displaced by criminal resurgence

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Post-first-game peace (one year after Streets of Rage 1)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Characters reminiscing at nightspot; Axel working as bodyguard; Blaze teaching dance classes; Adam in police force; domestic normalcy
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Low-threat interactions; celebratory atmosphere; characters dispersed geographically (Axel and Blaze left city)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Decayed urban environments; boarded-up buildings; visible street chaos; Adam's house in ruins; kidnapping photograph evidence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Beatings and looting in broad daylight; characters forced to reunite; immediate mobilization for rescue mission

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Progressive journey from Wood Oak City streets through various zones to island fortress; escalating enemy encounters; stage-by-stage advancement
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Coordinated team combat; wave-based enemy encounters; increasing difficulty and challenge intensity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mr. X and Shiva boss encounter; island fortress setting; helicopter arrival at conclusion
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Climactic battle; single ending path (deterministic resolution); escape via helicopter confirms liberation and restored order

environmental_storytelling.example: Wood Oak City streets show visible deterioration with boarded buildings and abandoned storefronts, communicating Syndicate control without exposition
Axel and Blaze having left the city after first game establishes peace interrupted; their return signals urgency. Adam's family home represents sanctuary violated
Journey from familiar urban streets → progressive environmental unfamiliarity → desolate island fortress mirrors escalating narrative stakes and physical separation from normal society
Photograph of Adam chained at Mr. X's feet functions as visual proof of threat without cutscene; door placement is environmental storytelling
Skate Hunter introduced as desperate caller then joins team; Max Thunder presented as willing ally. Visual/gameplay roles (Skate's speed, Max's power) reflect narrative personality

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Part-time bodyguard work; reminiscing at nightspot with friends; geographically absent from Wood Oak City
Immediately vows to help Blaze; personally commits to rescue mission; mobilizes for combat
Teaching dance classes; has relocated; celebrating with friends
Receives emergency call from Skate; immediately contacts Axel; takes leadership role in informing others of threat
Arrives home from school; discovers house ransacked; reaches out to Blaze for help
Joins rescue team despite youth; gameplay role offers speed advantage (rollerblades)
Seeks to aid Axel and Blaze proactively; joins rescue mission voluntarily; gameplay role reflects strength (slow but powerful)
Orchestrates return offensive; kidnaps Adam as leverage against protagonists; establishes island fortress; faces heroes only in final confrontation
Retakes streets; engages in visible criminal activity (beatings, looting) in daylight; deploys waves of diverse combat specialists
Captured and chained; used as narrative motivation; rescued in single ending path

notes: Streets of Rage 2 uses environmental design and character-driven narrative to communicate world-state shifts. The game visually establishes normalcy (peaceful nightspot, civilian employment) then dramatically shifts to urban decay and criminal occupation. Faction identity is conveyed primarily through environmental context (Syndicate-controlled streets) and character type variety rather than explicit visual sigils. Danger signals accumulate through enemy density and boss encounters. The narrative's single ending path reinforces deterministic victory and moral clarity. Character behavioral shifts (peaceful → mobilized) reflect plot progression. The journey from city to island fortress creates spatial storytelling that mirrors narrative escalation.
